title: RobustSAE: Adversarially Robust Sparse Autoencoders for Trustworthy Concept Interpretation
description: We propose RobustSAE, the first adversarially robust sparse autoencoder framework that addresses the 'interpretability illusion' problem identified by Li et al. (2025). RobustSAE introduces consistency regularization to enforce stable SAE representations under semantic perturbations, and an unsupervised robustness proxy to identify fragile features without adversarial training. This approach is orthogonal to concurrent diversity methods (Pan et al., ICML 2025; Xu et al., 2025), solving the distinct problem of adversarial robustness rather than feature coverage.
motivation: Recent work by Li et al. (2025) reveals a critical vulnerability: SAE concept representations are highly fragile to minimal adversarial perturbations that leave the underlying LLM behavior unchanged. This 'interpretability illusion' renders current SAEs unreliable for safety-critical applications like model monitoring and oversight. While concurrent papers address feature diversity via denoising (Pan et al., 2025) or MoE architectures (Xu et al., 2025), no existing work targets adversarial robustness in SAEs. This represents a critical gap in trustworthy interpretability.
proposed_approach: RobustSAE augments standard SAE training with two key innovations: (1) Consistency regularization that penalizes representation divergence between original and semantically-perturbed inputs, enforcing Lipschitz continuity in the encoder mapping. Unlike denoising for diversity which uses destructive noise, we use semantic-preserving perturbations (paraphrases, synonyms). (2) An unsupervised robustness proxy using gradient concentration analysis to identify potentially fragile features without requiring adversarial training data. This proxy serves as an early warning system for interpretability illusions.
key_innovations: First adversarially robust SAE training framework with consistency regularization
Unsupervised robustness proxy for identifying fragile features without adversarial data
Theoretical connection between consistency regularization and Lipschitz robustness
Explicit differentiation from diversity methods (robustness vs. coverage are orthogonal)

related_work.paper: Interpretability Illusions with Sparse Autoencoders (Li et al., 2025)
related_work.difference: Identifies the robustness problem but does not propose training solutions; we provide the first solution

related_work.paper: Beyond Sparsity: Improving Diversity in SAEs via Denoising Training (Pan et al., ICML 2025)
related_work.difference: Uses dropout/denoising for feature diversity/coverage; we target adversarial robustness; approaches are complementary

related_work.paper: Beyond Redundancy: Diverse Multi-Expert SAE (Xu et al., arXiv 2025)
related_work.difference: Uses MoE architecture for diversity; we address robustness; can be combined

related_work.paper: Scaling and Evaluating Sparse Autoencoders (Gao et al., 2024)
related_work.difference: Standard SAE training; no robustness consideration

related_work.paper: SAEBench: Comprehensive Benchmark for SAEs (Karvonen et al., 2025)
related_work.difference: Evaluation framework without robustness metrics; we extend with robustness evaluation

related_work.paper: Sparse Feature Circuits (Marks et al., ICLR 2024)
related_work.difference: Uses attribution for circuit discovery; we focus on training-time robustness

hypothesis: By incorporating consistency regularization into SAE training, we can significantly improve adversarial robustness metrics (>25% reduction in attack success rate) while maintaining reconstruction quality and interpretability. The unsupervised robustness proxy will show strong correlation (>0.6) with empirical adversarial vulnerability.

confirmed: RobustSAE shows >25% reduction in attack success rate vs. standard SAEs (p < 0.01)
Reconstruction quality maintained within 10% FVU of baseline
Unsupervised proxy achieves >0.6 Spearman correlation with empirical fragility
Robustness improvements generalize across attack types (suffix, replacement, population, individual)
refuted: No significant improvement in robustness metrics vs. baselines
Consistency regularization catastrophically degrades reconstruction (>30% FVU increase)
Robustness gains limited to specific attack scenarios only
Proxy shows no correlation with actual robustness
partial: Robustness improves but only for population-level attacks
Method works for small models but fails to scale to larger ones
Reconstruction-robustness tradeoff requires extensive hyperparameter tuning
Proxy works for some feature types but not others

datasets: Art & Science dataset (Li et al., 2025) for robustness evaluation
metrics: Robustness: Attack success rate, population overlap change, semantic stability
Standard: FVU, L0 sparsity, loss recovered
experiments.estimated_runtime: ~8 hours on NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB): 4h training + 2h robustness eval + 1h proxy validation + 1h downstream tasks

limitations: Consistency regularization increases training time by 2-3x
Perturbation strategy may not cover all adversarial attacks
Limited to moderately-sized models due to compute constraints
future_work: Evaluate against adaptive attacks aware of consistency training
Explore certified robustness (randomized smoothing) for SAEs
Study cross-model transfer of robust features
Apply RobustSAE to safety-critical monitoring applications
expected_contributions: First adversarially robust SAE training framework
Unsupervised robustness proxy for feature reliability assessment
Open-source implementation and pretrained robust SAEs
preliminary_sanity_checks: Robustness metrics improve with higher lambda_consist
Proxy scores rank features consistently across random seeds
Semantic perturbations preserve LLM output (validated via LLM judge)

distinction_from_concurrent_work.note: We explicitly address and differentiate from concurrent papers on SAE diversity
distinction_from_concurrent_work.beyond_sparsity_pan: Pan et al. (ICML 2025) uses denoising for diversity/coverage; we target adversarial robustness
distinction_from_concurrent_work.multi_expert_xu: Xu et al. (arXiv 2025) uses MoE for redundancy reduction; orthogonal to our approach
distinction_from_concurrent_work.relationship: Our robustness methods can be COMBINED with diversity methods for SAEs that are both diverse and robust
